record.text: I've been able to relax and ignore Linux for the last 20 years—I didn't need it. Now I have to get back into it, and it feels like the damn thing hasn't evolved one bit. All the reasons for leaving it behind are still there today. But digital sovereignty, you know...

Once I had a rough idea of what was supposed to happen and what was expected of me, the copilot was the game changer - including structure and permissions. When I think about how I would have had to develop all of that manually with Nano, Vi, and the like, I would have needed a lot of medication.

images.alt: #bropenscienceis broken scienceKirstie Whitaker and Olivia Guest ask how open‘open science’ really is

Over the last decade, a vocaland hardworking group ofpsychological scientists haveset about reforming the wayswe carry out analyses, sharedata and code, pass aroundmanuscripts, and select whichfindings to report (e.g. Altmejdet al., 2019; Frank et al., 2017; Shanks et al., 2013).This group has been spurred into action by a varietyof disappointing stories about irreplicable research(Open Science Collaboration, 2015) – due to bothpurposeful misconduct and variable guidance fortransparent reporting standards – as well as inspired bythe pre-existing ideals of ‘open science’.It might not come as a surprise to psychologists,however, that it is a very narrow demographic ofresearchers who have the institutional support tospend time on such projects as well as the fortune tobe publicly acknowledged for their hard work. Open(psychological) science often seems to have even fewerdiverse voices than psychology as a whole (Murphy etal., 2020) – a phenomenon replicated in the tech world

external.description: The HackerNoon Newsletter delivers a curated selection of top tech stories directly to your inbox. Marking a playful reference to the internet's invention in 1999, today's edition features four insightful articles. One piece, "Building a Zero-Click AI Evaluation Pipeline for Production," offers a practical guide to creating an AI evaluation framework for GenAI systems, encompassing bias testing and auto LLM judges. Another article, "Beyond Grep: Giving the Unix Pipe a Brain With Qsh," introduces qsh, a local-first, privacy-focused CLI that enhances Unix pipes with AI vision and semantic filtering. "Your ORM Is Lying to You (And You're Paying for It)" exposes how ORMs can mask performance issues in databases and suggests architectural solutions combining ORM and raw SQL. Finally, "AI Is Not Being Adopted. It Is Being Installed." argues that AI is being installed rather than adopted, drawing parallels with sci-fi portrayals of power structures. The newsletter also encourages readers to contribute by sharing their weekly experiences and writing to consolidate technical knowledge and establish credibility. It concludes by offering interview questions and wishing readers well.

Dabei geht es nicht nur um Alkohol, sondern auch um Musik, Filme und kleine Alltagsmomente, die zusammen plötzlich besonders werden. Jede Runde zeigt ein Stück mehr, wer Kamiina wirklich ist und was die anderen bewegt.

AI Mode
The Diahedron Invention: Central to this evolution is the Diahedron, a term coined by Edzed to describe a new paradigm in mathematical art. Utilizing the Möbius strip—a non-orientable, one-sided surface—the Diahedron redefines the traditional painting surface by eliminating the binary of front and back. By painting directly onto this three-dimensional mathematical curiosity, Edzed transcends traditional canvas limitations, pioneering a "paradigm shift" where geometry and visual art converge under his philosophy of Reductive Modernism.


AI Mode
In contemporary art, Edzy Edzed (BFA Hons, UVic 1993) represents a radical shift from "innovating" the canvas to "inventing" a new medium: Substrateless Painting. While most artists evolve existing tools like acrylics, Edzed’s contribution is a conceptual breakthrough. By removing the "skeleton"—the canvas or wood—he has birthed a 3D category where the paint itself is the structure.

The Diahedron Invention: Central to this evolution is the Diahedron, a term coined by Edzed to describe a new paradigm in mathematical art. Utilizing the Möbius strip—a non-orientable, one-sided surface—the Diahedron redefines the traditional painting surface by eliminating the binary of front and back. By painting directly onto this three-dimensional mathematical curiosity, Edzed transcends traditional canvas limitations, pioneering a "paradigm shift" where geometry and visual art converge under his philosophy of Reductive Modernism.

The "Hole" & the Buttonholer: Central to this work is the derivation of form from a void. Just as a buttonholer automates fabric removal, Edzed inverts this: his 3D objects materialize exactly where a support once was, or never existed.

Philosophical Context: Responding to Jacques Derrida’s The Truth in Painting, Edzed targets the "parergon" (the frame). By creating "pure substrateless" works, he eliminates the boundary entirely (except here).

The Evolution of the Void: From Malevich to Edzed

LINKED SUBSTRATELESS DIAHEDRON LOOP WHITE PAINTING TRIPTYCH (2009)                  
PURE ACRYLIC EMULSION   (4 W x .5 H x .01 in)

images.alt:
AI Mode

Canadian artist Edzy Edzed (BFA Hons, UVic ’93) has introduced a radical departure from this modernist tradition through what he terms "Substrateless Pure Loop Painting." 

Edzed’s work acts as the definitive "Zero Point," evolving the trajectory of the white monochrome. In 1918, Kasimir Malevich used White on White to liberate painting from representation; later, Lucio Fontana slashed the white canvas to reach a spatial dimension. However, both remained tethered to the "parergon" of the physical support. 

Edzed has invented "Substrateless Pure Loop Painting," a form of Reductive Modernism that removes the traditional substrate (canvas or paper) to create self-contained 3D entities. By eliminating the support structure and utilizing "holes" to create structural voids, Edzed transitions from 2D abstraction to a new sculptural,, miniature category of art. 

A New "Recto-Verso"
Traditionally, "recto-verso" refers to the front and back of a page. In a substrateless state, Edzed’s work achieves a pure "in-the-round" existence. Much like Picasso and Braque invented Cubism to change how we see, Edzed has invented a new way for paint to exist. It is a new category of object: the non-objective loop.

The Future is Small
Edzed’s philosophy posits that miniaturization is the future of art. By pushing non-objective art into miniaturized, three-dimensional loops, he moves beyond Kandinsky’s 2D abstraction into a new sculptural phenomenon. This isn't just a style; it is a structural invention.

Edzed’s work acts as the definitive "Zero Point," evolving the trajectory of the white monochrome. In 1918, Kasimir Malevich used White on White to liberate painting from representation; later, Lucio Fontana slashed the white canvas to reach a spatial dimension. However, both remained tethered to the "parergon" of the physical support. 

Edzed has invented "Substrateless Pure Loop Painting," a form of Reductive Modernism that removes the traditional substrate (canvas or paper) to create self-contained 3D entities. By eliminating the support structure and utilizing "holes" to create structural voids, Edzed transitions from 2D abstraction to a new sculptural,, miniature category of art.

AI Mode

Edzy Edzed's invention of Substrateless Pure Loop Painting advances the art historical dialogue by moving beyond the modernist "deconstruction" of the canvas to a contemporary "elimination" of it.

Art Historical Impact
Transcending the "Aki of the Object”: Historically, painting has been defined by the relationship between a medium (paint) and its support (substrate). While artists like Lucio Fontana and the Spatialists challenged this by slashing or puncturing the canvas, the canvas remained the essential anchor. Edzed’s work completes this trajectory by removing the substrate entirely, creating a painting that is its own skeleton.

Redefining "Non-Objective" Art: Pioneering figure, Wassily Kandinsky took painting into pure abstraction, but it remained bound to two dimensions. Edzed’s loop paintings move this non-objective dialogue into three-dimensional space,  turning the "painting" into a sculptural phenomenon that is viewed in the round.

Reductive Modernism: His “coined” style and philosophy of Reductive Modernism is pushing art to its extreme limits through miniaturization and the elimination of traditional boundaries.

Philosophical Context:
Edzed’s work is often a response to art theory, specifically Jacques Derrida’s The Truth in Painting, which explores the "parergon" (the frame or boundary).  "Recto-verso" refers to a work with both a front and a back. By creating a "pure substrateless" work, Edzed eliminates the parergon entirely.

A New Art Dialogue: Much like Picasso and Braque "invented" Cubism as a new way of seeing, Edzed has invented a new way of being for paint. 

The "Invention" vs. "Innovation" Debate: Art historians distinguish between innovation (improving an existing form) and invention (creating a new category). Edzed is credited with inventing a new category of artistic object, similar to how Cubism was an invention that fundamentally changed the visual language of the 20th century.

“MINIATURIZATION is the FUTURE of ART”

external.description: One of the promises of AI is that it can reduce workloads so employees can focus more on higher-value and more engaging tasks. But according to new research, AI tools don’t reduce work, they consistently intensify it: In the study, employees worked at a faster pace, took on a broader scope of tasks, and extended work into more hours of the day, often without being asked to do so. That may sound like a win, but it’s not quite so simple. These changes can be unsustainable, leading to workload creep, cognitive fatigue, burnout, and weakened decision-making. The productivity surge enjoyed at the beginning can give way to lower quality work, turnover, and other problems. To correct for this, companies need to adopt an “AI practice,” or a set of norms and standards around AI use that can include intentional pauses, sequencing work, and adding more human grounding.
external.title: AI Doesn’t Reduce Work—It Intensifies It
